narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application. Your family comes from a high income bracket, which is a positive factor in many admissions considerations. However, the admissions model, designed to predict first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated an 80.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure triggered the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass on their first try. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which was the top influence pushing toward a failure prediction—far below the average 5.53 decile, signaling weaker performance relative to peers in those later years. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 1st, even lower than the typical 5.71 average, which further reinforced the model's concern about your early academic standing in school. While your high family income provided some counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward success, it wasn't strong enough to offset those lower decile rankings. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your position in the bottom deciles for both early and later school years stands out as unusually low, which is why it dominated the model's thinking for your profile. Your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag on bar passage patterns seen in similar cases. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor—many students in your position have succeeded by targeting academic consistency early on. You might also explore targeted bar prep resources or admissions appeals with updated transcripts to demonstrate growth. Keep pushing; your other strengths position you well for another shot.
